title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz Again; Pakistan Mediation Under Acute Strain; Af-Pak Non-Escalation Holds at Day 10
detail: Iran reversed its brief announcement of reopening the Strait of Hormuz on April 19, reimposing restrictions on the waterway after the United States refused to lift its naval blockade of Iranian ports and seized an Iranian-flagged cargo vessel. Iranian military forces opened fire on at least one vessel attempting to transit the strait; no tankers passed through on April 19. Iran's Foreign Ministry announced it saw no basis for a second round of US-Iran talks as long as the blockade remained in place, directly undercutting Pakistan's intense diplomatic effort of the preceding week. Trump nonetheless announced that US Vice President JD Vance, Special Envoy Steve Witkoff, and Jared Kushner would travel to Islamabad on Monday, April 20, for a second round of talks — but Iran stated it had no plans to join. For the Afghanistan-Pakistan theater, April 19 marked the 10th consecutive day without major cross-border military incidents since the Urumqi non-escalation commitment took effect on April 9. The relative quiet along the Durand Line continued unbroken. However, the Strait of Hormuz closure carried indirect consequences for Afghanistan: roughly one-fifth of global oil trade transits the strait, along with humanitarian supplies destined for Afghanistan through Iran. Pakistan's mounting role as primary US-Iran mediator continued to divert its diplomatic bandwidth from preparations for the second round of China-mediated Af-Pak talks in Urumqi, originally described in the April 9 communiqué as due at 'end of April.' Taliban officials in Kabul monitored the US-Iran standoff closely, aware that Pakistan's diplomatic status — and its political capital for making Af-Pak concessions — was directly tied to outcomes in the Iran track. Separately, local tribal elders in Nari district, Kunar, and Nuristan's border communities confirmed that a local agreement had been reached with Pakistani border forces in Chitral to allow civilian vehicles to transit the Kamdesh–Barg-e-Matal corridor, reopening a lifeline to approximately 100,000 cut-off residents in Nuristan's Bargematal and Kamdesh districts. Taliban officials confirmed the route reopened but said the Taliban government played no role in brokering the local deal, underscoring the grassroots nature of the agreement.

media.caption: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz again, no tankers pass on April 19 as US-Iran ceasefire nears expiry
